sourceFiles.name: thermal-system

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A thermal control fragment specifying when a system should activate cooling and when it should enter emergency shutdown based on temperature.
- Operational domain: Likely industrial/electrical/embedded thermal management.
- Model scope: Small model fragment containing two requirement definitions and one part definition with temperature-related attributes.
- Known scope limitations: No behaviour model, no state machine, no verification cases, no decomposition hierarchy beyond one part definition, no interfaces/relationships/packages beyond the single package shown. Checks requiring those artefacts are assessed as not evidenced in this fragment and only explicit text can be judged.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The two requirements were examined for identical or near-identical wording and they express different trigger conditions and different actions ("emergency shutdown" at 80C vs "cooling fan shall turn on" at 75C), so no requirement duplication is present.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  The model contains only one `part def` (`CoolingSystem`) and no `item def` or alternative definitions of the same entity, so there is no duplicated structural definition to compare.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or specialization relationships are present in the fragment, so no redundant relationships were found.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  No state machines, action definitions, sequences, or message exchange models are present, so there is no behavioural duplication to assess in this fragment.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::CoolingSystem::attribute emergencyShutdownThresholdC; thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        The numeric thresholds are defined twice: once in requirement text and again as explicit part attributes with the same values. This restates the same constraint information in two places without any shown derivation, binding, or redefinition mechanism linking them.
Evidence:       Requirement `EmergencyShutdown`: "The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius." Attribute `emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;`. Requirement `CoolingFanActivation`: "The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 75 degrees Celsius." Attribute `coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 75.0;`.
Score:          0.72  -  Minor-to-moderate redundancy; the model remains understandable, but duplicated threshold values create maintenance and divergence risk.
Confidence:     HIGH
Risk:           If a threshold changes in one place and not the other, requirements, implementation parameters, and downstream verification may silently diverge, causing incorrect design assumptions or invalid test expectations.
Recommendation: Keep the authoritative threshold in one place and reference it from the other. Either retain the requirements as source authority and derive/bind model parameters from them, or keep the attributes as design parameters and rewrite requirements to reference named parameters or add explicit trace/derivation relations between the duplicated values.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  No decomposition hierarchy, intermediate pass-through blocks, empty packages, or parent actions with single redundant sub-actions are present beyond the single package and single part definition.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated architectural elements suggesting intentional design redundancy (e.g., redundant hardware paths) are present; the only redundancy identified is informational duplication of threshold values, which appears structural rather than fault-tolerance-related.

CHECK PASSED: 8. States that are never activated  -  Although the criterion mentions unreachable behaviour, this fragment contains no behaviour/state model; additionally, the thresholds are ordered consistently (fan at 75C before shutdown at 80C), so no explicit redundant never-activated state is evidenced.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.72 | HIGH | Temperature threshold values are duplicated in both requirement text and part attributes without explicit linkage. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.72
Model Status: Notable gaps - address before finalising design decisions
